museum.name: Gaming Time Machine

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight notable games from the year, led by month-specific anchors when the evidence supports them.

items.title: Lemmings
items.body: DMA Design and Psygnosis turn tiny walkers into a national puzzle obsession.
items.sourceNote: 1991 Amiga release is secure; exact regional retail dates should be checked.

items.title: Street Fighter II: The World Warrior
items.body: Capcom reshapes the fighting game around characters, queues and mastery.
items.sourceNote: March 1991 Japanese arcade release is commonly cited.

items.title: Sonic the Hedgehog
items.body: Sega's mascot arrives in PAL regions and North America within days.
items.sourceNote: Wikipedia cites PAL June 21 and North America June 23.

items.title: Final Fantasy IV
items.body: Square uses Nintendo's 16-bit machine for a more dramatic RPG language.
items.sourceNote: Japanese release date is commonly cited.

items.title: Another World
items.body: Eric Chahi's cinematic platformer makes silence, animation and danger feel authored.
items.sourceNote: 1991 is secure; exact month varies by platform/source.

items.title: Streets of Rage
items.body: Sega gives home players a stylish brawler for the Mega Drive era.
items.sourceNote: 1991 is secure; exact regional dates vary.

items.title: The Legend of Zelda: A Link to the Past
items.body: Nintendo returns Zelda to a top-down world with greater scale and polish.
items.sourceNote: Nintendo Life cites November 21, 1991 in Japan.

items.title: Mega Man 4
items.body: Capcom extends its late-8-bit action craft in Japan.
items.sourceNote: December 1991 Japanese release is commonly cited.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Machines, formats and buying context around the exhibit month.

items.title: Game Gear arrives in the West
items.body: Sega's colour handheld becomes part of the British handheld comparison, even as battery life remains part of the bargain.
items.sourceNote: April 1991 is commonly cited for North America/Europe; regional retail timing should be checked for exact UK claims.

items.title: Super NES reaches North America
items.body: Nintendo's 16-bit machine changes the US conversation, while UK players are still mainly watching previews and import pages.
items.sourceNote: North American 1991 launch is secure; UK/EU release follows in 1992.

items.title: Mega Drive becomes Sega's personality machine
items.body: Sonic's arrival makes the Mega Drive feel less like imported arcade muscle and more like a full identity.
items.sourceNote: Sonic release context.

items.title: PC Engine Duo appears in Japan
items.body: NEC's CD/cartridge hybrid points toward a future where storage formats are becoming part of the story.
items.sourceNote: September 1991 Japanese launch is commonly cited.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: How players found information before search, streams and social feeds.

items.title: BBS life is still specialist
items.body: Gaming discussion mostly travels through dial-up bulletin boards, school friends and magazine letters pages.

items.title: Shareware is becoming a PC habit
items.body: Before the web becomes ordinary, disks, BBS uploads and copied archives give PC games a different route into homes.

items.title: Cheats arrive slowly
items.body: Codes and maps still feel like information smuggled through magazines or friends rather than instantly searchable knowledge.

items.title: Import knowledge is social capital
items.body: Knowing what Japan or America already has can make a UK reader feel months ahead and months behind at the same time.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A short atmospheric reading of the month from the player side of history.

items.title: The future had a mascot
items.body: Sonic made the 16-bit argument feel fast, blue and designed for television adverts.

items.title: The arcade came home unevenly
items.body: Street Fighter II was the cabinet everyone talked about, but its home future still had to be imagined.

items.title: A puzzle game could own a room
items.body: Lemmings made one Amiga screen feel like a tiny society in crisis.

items.title: Britain was between generations
items.body: Mega Drive was visible, SNES was desirable, and many players still lived with 8-bit machines at home.
